activities.title: Project Administration
activities.intro_text: Set up the administrative backbone of the change project — governance rhythm, document repository, stakeholder access, and reporting cadence.
activities.full_description: Before diagnostic work begins, establish the working infrastructure that will carry the project through all phases. This includes setting up a shared document repository with appropriate access permissions, agreeing on a governance rhythm with the project sponsor (frequency and format of status updates, steering committee cadence, escalation paths), defining how change management deliverables will be version-controlled and reviewed, and creating the initial project tracker. This card is also the ongoing home for administrative tasks that arise throughout the project — updating the RAID log, scheduling recurring meetings, maintaining distribution lists, and managing consultant access to client systems. While not intellectually demanding, neglecting project administration creates friction that slows every other activity.

activities.title: Get Prerequisite Items
activities.intro_text: Obtain foundational data from the project lead to define the scope of the change.
activities.full_description: Ask the clients internal project lead for a list of populations and a list of the people involved: their function, department and email address

activities.title: Assess Sponsorship in a Structured Interview
activities.intro_text: Identify the executive sponsor and any co-sponsors for this initiative
activities.full_description: What does success look like to them personally? What are they willing to do visibly? What resources will they defend when competing priorities emerge? How have they handled change resistance before? It's possible to use a sponsor readiness matrix (Prosci's CLARC model, which tracks development of sponsorship over time).
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts** (e.g., M365 Copilot), probe specifically whether the sponsor has personal experience with the technology and whether they will use it visibly. For **process changes**, focus on whether the sponsor understands operational impact at the frontline level. For **restructuring or mergers**, assess the sponsor's willingness to have difficult conversations about role changes, and whether they have the authority to make decisions about affected populations. Add questions about budget authority if the initiative requires significant investment.

activities.title: Stakeholder Identification and Influence Mapping
activities.intro_text: Obtain the organisational chart for all affected business units
activities.full_description: Structured Interviews with Senior leaders (and their direct reports). Influence can be identified by asking the question: When something needs to get done here that isn't straight-forward, who do people actually go to? Document this as a stakeholder map with axes of influence and disposition (supportive, neutral, resistant)
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts**, ensure you identify the IT stakeholders who control deployment, licensing, and security configuration — they can block adoption regardless of business support. For **process changes**, map the entire value chain to identify upstream and downstream stakeholders who may not be obvious. For **mergers and restructuring**, include stakeholders from both merging entities, and pay special attention to people who may lose power or status. Add questions about political dynamics and territory if the change crosses organisational boundaries.

activities.title: Impact Analysis at the Role Level
activities.intro_text: Identify all distinct roles affected by the change (not just job titles — functional roles)
activities.full_description: This is where we need to understand how specific roles will change. Workflows will disappear, which gain new steps which require fundamentally different skills. The tool here is a structured impact analysis, typically a matrix. Mapping the gap between the current workflow and the future state workflow per role is the raw material for everything that's designed later.
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts** (e.g., M365 Copilot), map impact per application per role — e.g., Finance uses Excel heavily, Communications uses Word and Outlook. For **process changes**, walk the end-to-end process with process owners and identify every handoff point that changes. For **restructuring**, the impact analysis must include emotional and identity impacts (loss of team, loss of role, change of manager), not just workflow changes. Add questions about seasonal business cycles if the change has timing implications.

activities.title: Assess Current State Capability Baseline
activities.intro_text: Define what capabilities (skills, knowledge, behaviours) are relevant to assess for this change
activities.full_description: What can people actually do right now with their existing tools? What are their current abilities and frustrations? Surveys can work here.
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts**, assess current digital literacy levels — not just familiarity with the specific tool, but general comfort with technology. Include questions about the current tool equivalent (e.g., "How do you currently use Excel?" before introducing Copilot in Excel). For **process changes**, assess current process compliance — if people aren't following the current process, introducing a new one has different implications. For **restructuring**, assess leadership and change management capabilities of managers who will need to lead their teams through the transition.

activities.title: Organisational Change History and Scar Tissue Assessment
activities.intro_text: Identify the major changes the organisation has undergone in the last 2–3 years
activities.full_description: Understand the narrative of previous changes, especially the ones that went badly. Interview long-tenured staff and middle managers, asking specifically what changed in the last two to three years, what went well, and what was painful. What was promised and what was not delivered.
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts**, specifically ask about previous technology implementations — did the last system go-live go smoothly? Was training adequate? Were workarounds needed? For **process changes**, ask about previous process improvement initiatives (e.g., Lean, Six Sigma) — were they sustained or did old habits return? For **mergers and restructuring**, this assessment is critical: ask about previous rounds of redundancies, restructuring, or leadership changes and the emotional residue they left. The scar tissue from these events can dominate the response to the current change.

activities.title: Assess Middle Management Readiness and Willingness
activities.intro_text: Identify all middle managers whose teams are affected by the change
activities.full_description: This determines whether the change actually propagates or dies. Possible to go into small group conversations.
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts**, assess managers' own digital competence — a manager who doesn't use the tool cannot credibly coach their team. Add questions like "Will you use this tool yourself?" and "How comfortable are you with technology?" For **process changes**, probe whether the manager was involved in designing the new process — involvement increases ownership. For **restructuring**, this conversation is extremely sensitive: managers may themselves be affected. Assess whether they know their own future before asking them to support their team's transition. Adjust the tone and framing accordingly.

activities.title: Create Resistance Risk Profile
activities.intro_text: Compile findings from Activities 1–6 into a single working document
activities.full_description:
For each affected population you should be able to articulate what they stand to lose in terms of:
- status
- competence
- autonomy
- workload
- predictability
and what they might gain.What does their prior change experience predispose them toward? Which structural factors, like manager support, sponsor visibility, communication trust, either amplify or dampen their resistance? This profile should drive the intervention design.
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts** (especially AI/automation), explicitly assess fear of job displacement — this is often the dominant resistance factor even if unstated. Add questions about whether roles will be eliminated. For **process changes**, the primary resistance factor is often workload — new processes almost always require more effort initially. For **restructuring**, loss of status and autonomy dominate. Tailor the loss analysis dimensions to match the type of change.

activities.title: Create a Change Strategy Document
activities.intro_text: Draft the change strategy document structure: organisation context, change difficulty analysis, intervention plan, success criteria
activities.full_description: Put the findings into a coherent argument: Here's the organisation we're changing. Here's what makes this change specifically difficult for them. Here's what we'll do about it and here's how we'll know how it's working. It needs to contain named populations with specific intervention plans.
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts**, the strategy should address the deployment sequence (who gets it first) and its change management implications. For **process changes**, focus on embedding the change into standard operating procedures so it's structurally reinforced. For **mergers**, the strategy must address cultural integration — not just process alignment. The strategy document format stays the same, but the weight given to each section shifts with the change type.

activities.title: Design a Stakeholder Engagement Plan
activities.intro_text: Review the stakeholder map (from Activity 2) and resistance risk profile (from Activity 7)
activities.full_description:
Different from the communication plan and operates at a different altitude. Build a coalition before the change becomes visible to the broader population.  Your one-on-one conversations with key influencers. Supportive leaders with talking points and early access Engage resistance stakeholders early enough. Their concerns can shape the approach rather than ambush it. Stakeholder engagement plans should have a cadence:
- Who to meet with
- When
- What you need from them at each stage
- What they need from you
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts**, include IT leadership and security/compliance stakeholders in the engagement plan — they often control access and configuration. For **process changes**, engage process owners and quality teams who will need to update documentation. For **mergers**, the engagement plan must address stakeholders from both entities and be sensitive to power dynamics. Add engagement with works councils or unions if applicable to the jurisdiction.

activities.title: Design the Training Architecture
activities.intro_text: Map the gap between current capability (Activity 4) and required future-state capability (Activity 3) per role
activities.full_description: Which modality is for which populations? What points in the deployment timeline with what prerequisites measured by outcome. This is obvious. A finance team needs deep training in Co-Pilot in Excel, while communications need Word and Outlook.
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts**, design role-specific training paths (e.g., Finance team on Copilot in Excel, Communications on Word and Outlook). Include a safe practice environment so people can experiment without fear of breaking things. For **process changes**, training often needs to include "unlearning" — explicitly addressing why the old way is changing and what to stop doing. For **restructuring**, training may include leadership and change management skills for managers, not just operational skills.

activities.title: Design Communication with Message Architecture
activities.intro_text: Define the communication audiences (may differ from stakeholder groups — broader reach)
activities.full_description:
Messages need to be sequenced in a way that matches the audience's psychological journey not the project's technical milestones. 
First communication: answer why? Why this change? Why now? Why us? 
Second communication: What will change specifically for me? 
Third communication: We address the how to use the new thing. 
Managers should hear about things before the teams do.
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts**, the "What changes for me?" message must be very specific and practical — avoid generic "this exciting new tool" language. Show before/after examples of actual workflows. For **process changes**, emphasise the rationale for the change and acknowledge what was good about the old way. For **mergers and restructuring**, communication is the most critical intervention — address job security concerns head-on and early, and avoid corporate euphemisms.

activities.title: Get Technical Deployment Timeline and Dependencies
activities.intro_text: Obtain the full technical deployment timeline from the IT/technical project manager
activities.full_description: Map every milestone that has a downstream ACM implication. Will the pilot environments be available? The tenant configuration is finalised. The rollback plan if Go Live fails Build the plan against the technical timeline and make sure to be  apprised of changes. Also get the actual technical configuration.
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts**, this activity is central — the entire ACM plan is built around the technical deployment sequence. Get into the detail: licence provisioning, conditional access policies, security configurations, integration testing. For **process changes**, the "deployment" is the process switchover — understand what triggers the transition from old to new process and whether there's a parallel running period. For **restructuring**, the timeline centres on organisational announcements, contractual changes, and regulatory consultation periods.

activities.title: Establish the Measurement Framework
activities.intro_text: Define metrics at three levels:
activities.full_description:
Focus on adoption metrics as the first level; that is, utilisation telemetry. Second-level proficiency metrics assessment scores, task completion, quality, error rates. Third level: Business impact metrics. Output quality indicators. You need Partnerships with business stakeholders here.  
Put these measurements into your change strategy document.
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts** (especially M365), rich telemetry is often available through admin centres and adoption dashboards — leverage these rather than building custom solutions. For **process changes**, metrics may need to be manually collected (e.g., audit of process compliance). For **restructuring**, measurement often focuses on employee engagement surveys, retention rates, and productivity indicators rather than system telemetry.

activities.title: Develop Resistance Management Approach
activities.intro_text: Review the resistance risk profile (from Activity 7)
activities.full_description:
In the diagnostic was a resistance risk profile. This needs to be operationalized. For each resistance cluster identified, design a specific intervention and be precise about the mechanism. 
Example: If resistance is rooted in competence, anxiety (people fear looking incompetent withg the new tool)  Intervention is safe practise environments with psychological safety. Not more communications about how great the feature is. 
Example 2: If resistance is structural, for example the change is generally making someone's job harder without a compensating benefit, the intervention might be escalating that finding to governance as a design problem rather than an adoption problem. The resistance management approach should:
1. Name the pattern
2. Attribute the cause
3. Specify the intervention
4. Assign ownership
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts** (especially AI), competence anxiety and job displacement fear are the dominant patterns — design interventions around psychological safety, safe practice spaces, and honest conversations about how roles will evolve (not just "your job is safe"). For **process changes**, resistance often manifests as reverting to old processes — the intervention must address both motivation and structural reinforcement. For **restructuring**, resistance may be entirely legitimate grief — interventions should acknowledge this rather than trying to overcome it.

activities.title: Set Up the Change Network (Champions)
activities.intro_text: Define the champion role: expectations, time commitment, benefits, and duration
activities.full_description:
Identify and recruit change agents like champions. It should be people who carry peer credibility and can serve as local translators for the change. 
The set-up work involves:
- formal recruitment
- asking them
- explaining the role
- securing their manager's support for the time commitment
Equipping: give them early access, give them deeper context, and a direct line back to you for intelligence. 
Establish a rhythm for check-in. These should be regular check-ins where they report ground-level sentiment and you provide them with updated talking points and troubleshooting guidance. This change network becomes the distributing sensing apparatus during deployment. 
Identify the one (plus backup) person that will keep on engaging champions beyond consulting horizon and will check up on sustaining that change. Who "carries the banner?"
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts**, champions should be people who will use the tool daily and can demonstrate practical use cases to peers — not just tech-savvy people, but people who understand the work. For **process changes**, champions should ideally be people who were involved in designing the new process. For **restructuring**, the champion concept may need to be reframed as "change agents" or "transition guides" given the sensitivity of the change.

activities.title: Negotiate Governance Integration
activities.intro_text: Identify the existing programme governance structure (steering committee, project board, working groups)
activities.full_description: Make sure that ACM has a formal seat in the programme's governance structure. Which decisions can an ACM lead make autonomously? What constitutes an ACM risk that triggers escalation.
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **large technology rollouts**, governance integration is critical because technical decisions (e.g., feature suppression, security restrictions) directly affect adoption — the ACM lead must be able to influence these decisions. For **process changes**, governance may sit with operational leadership rather than IT. For **restructuring**, governance is typically very senior and politically charged — the ACM lead may need to report through a more senior intermediary rather than attending directly.

activities.title: Set Up Dedicated Teams Channels
activities.intro_text: Create or request creation of the following Teams channels:
activities.full_description:
-Sponsors 
- Pilot participants
- Champions
- Managers
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts**, the pilot participant channel is especially important — it becomes the primary feedback loop during pilot. For **process changes**, consider a "Process Q&A" channel where people can ask how to handle edge cases. For **restructuring**, channels must be very carefully managed — consider whether open channels are appropriate given the sensitivity, and whether private conversations would be more appropriate for some groups.

activities.title: Activate the Communication Sequence
activities.intro_text: Confirm the deployment date is firm with the technical team
activities.full_description:
Communicate at least 48 to 72 hours before anything goes live. It's far enough ahead to allow questions to surface through the management layer. 
Also, what are the champions getting back? Identify message failures that need immediate correction.
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts**, the day-one communication must be highly practical: "Open this app. Click here. Try this." Avoid generic enthusiasm. For **process changes**, communicate both what to start doing and what to stop doing. For **restructuring**, day-one communication is typically handled by senior leadership directly — the ACM consultant's role is to prepare and coach the leaders, not to send the communication.

activities.title: Execute Training as per Architecture
activities.intro_text: Confirm training schedule with all participants and their managers
activities.full_description: Awareness first, then skill building, then applied practise.
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts**, training must include hands-on practice in a safe environment, not just demonstrations. Consider recording sessions for on-demand replay. For **process changes**, training should include "day in the life" scenarios showing the new process end-to-end. For **restructuring**, training may focus on leadership skills, resilience, or new role orientation rather than tool/process skills.

activities.title: Shift to Active Field Support
activities.intro_text: Brief champions on their active support role during deployment
activities.full_description:
They provide peer-level help that carries less stigma than asking IT support or attending other trainings. They collect ground-level intelligence about what's working and what's confusing. They model productive use visibly within their teams. 
Your job is to maintain the cadence of Champion Check-ins, daily or every other day during the first two weeks of deployment. 
Provide support within hours not days.
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts**, champions should sit with their teams and demonstrate real use cases in real work — not contrived examples. For **process changes**, champions should be the go-to people for "how do I handle this edge case?" questions. For **restructuring**, field support is more about emotional support and answering "what happens to me?" questions — champions need coaching on how to handle these conversations.

activities.title: Run Support Infrastructure
activities.intro_text: Confirm dedicated help desk queue is operational and staffed
activities.full_description:
Whatever model has been designed:
- a dedicated help desk queue
- drop-in clinics or floor walking support
- Teams channel for questions
It needs to be visibly operational from the moment access goes live. 
People who hit a problem and find nothing will form a narrative of abandonment that persists long after support improves. Over-resource the first week deliberately then scale back on demand data.
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts**, ensure support staff have access to the same version and configuration that users have — nothing undermines credibility faster than support not being able to reproduce the user's experience. For **process changes**, support may need to come from process SMEs, not IT. For **restructuring**, "support" may be HR drop-in sessions or manager Q&A forums rather than a technical help desk.

activities.title: Monitor Adoption Telemetry Against Baseline
activities.intro_text: Activate monitoring dashboards and reports as designed in Activity 13
activities.full_description: Compare current numbers with baseline analysis
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts** (especially M365), leverage built-in adoption dashboards (e.g., Microsoft Adoption Score, Copilot Dashboard). For **process changes**, telemetry may not exist — use audit data, observation, or spot checks. For **restructuring**, monitor engagement survey data, attrition rates, and absenteeism as proxies for adoption health.

activities.title: Conduct Active Resistance Management
activities.intro_text: Activate the resistance management interventions designed in Activity 14
activities.full_description:
Pre-identified resistance clusters will now manifest as behaviour. 
Distinguish between productive resistance (People identifying genuine problems that should be escalated to the technical team as design feedback) And defensive (resistance, people protecting established habits or status.)
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts**, watch for "shadow IT" — people finding workarounds to avoid the new tool. This is a strong signal of either poor usability or inadequate training. For **process changes**, resistance often looks like non-compliance — people simply continuing to do things the old way. For **restructuring**, resistance may manifest as disengagement, increased sick leave, or talented people leaving — these are lagging indicators and by the time you see them, the damage is done. Focus on leading indicators like manager reports and champion intelligence.

activities.title: Run Rapid Check-Act Loops
activities.intro_text: Synthesise findings from all sensing channels: champion reports, support tickets, telemetry data, resistance observations, manager feedback
activities.full_description: A few days into and during deployment synthesise what you're seeing across all sensing channels. Identify two or three most significant gaps between expected and actual adoption Diagnose probable causes, design adjustments, and implement them within the same week. Document what you changed and why in a log.
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts**, the check-act loop should include a direct line to the technical team for configuration or feature issues that emerge during deployment. For **process changes**, loop in process owners who can authorise process adjustments. For **restructuring**, the check-act loop must include HR for people-related issues and legal if contractual or regulatory implications arise.

activities.title: Assess Champion Network Health
activities.intro_text: Conduct a structured review of the champion network: who is still active? Who has disengaged?
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts**, champion networks often need to persist beyond initial deployment as new features are released. For **process changes**, champions may transition into process owners or SMEs. For **restructuring**, the "champion" role may evolve into a peer support or wellbeing ambassador role during the transition period.

activities.title: Run Programme Level Check (Strategic Review)
activities.intro_text: Synthesise data from all sources: telemetry, interview findings, training effectiveness, champion reports, support ticket trends, resistance pattern e
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts**, the strategic review should include a comparison of adoption patterns against industry benchmarks if available. For **process changes**, include a process compliance audit. For **restructuring**, the strategic review must address cultural integration progress, not just operational metrics.

activities.title: Design the Reinforcement Plan
activities.intro_text: Segment the user population by adoption maturity: advanced users, competent users, struggling users, non-users
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts**, reinforcement often includes showcasing real examples of how colleagues are using the tool productively — social proof is powerful. For **process changes**, reinforcement may mean embedding the process into standard checklists or quality reviews. For **restructuring**, reinforcement is about embedding new team norms and working relationships — less about skills and more about culture.

activities.title: Identify and Escalate Systemic Barriers
activities.intro_text: Review all accumulated data for issues that are design, policy, or infrastructure problems — not adoption problems
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts** (especially M365 Copilot), common barriers include overly restrictive security policies, data access limitations, licensing issues, and missing integrations. For **process changes**, barriers include legacy systems that don't support the new process, or conflicting KPIs that incentivise old behaviours. For **restructuring**, barriers include unresolved reporting line ambiguities, duplicate roles, or contractual constraints.

activities.title: Begin Knowledge Transfer
activities.intro_text: Identify internal owners for ongoing adoption management:
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts**, ensure IT has the skills and tools to continue monitoring adoption telemetry. Ensure L&D has ownership of training materials and a plan for updating them as the technology evolves. For **process changes**, the process owner should take ownership of compliance monitoring and continuous improvement. For **restructuring**, knowledge transfer should include handover of any outstanding people issues to HR.

activities.title: State Resistance Profile and Adjust
activities.intro_text: Revisit the original resistance risk profile (Activity 7) and compare predictions to actual outcomes
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts**, watch for resistance that transformed from "I can't use it" (competence) to "I don't want to use it" (motivation) — this often signals poor user experience or lack of perceived value. For **process changes**, persistent non-compliance may indicate the new process is genuinely inferior in some situations. For **restructuring**, lingering resistance may indicate unresolved grief or real issues with the new structure that need addressing.

activities.title: Codify What Worked into Organisational Infrastructure
activities.intro_text: Identify successful patterns and behaviours that need to be structurally embedded (not dependent on project energy or champion effort)
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts**, codification includes ensuring the tool is part of standard operating procedures, included in onboarding, and that usage expectations are clear. For **process changes**, update process documentation, quality checklists, and audit procedures. For **restructuring**, codification means formalising new reporting lines, governance structures, and decision-making authorities in organisational documentation.

activities.title: Create ACM Programme Retrospective
activities.intro_text: Schedule a retrospective session with the core ACM team, programme manager, and key stakeholders
activities.particularisation_guidance: For **technology rollouts**, the retrospective should include a technology-specific section: was the product mature enough? Were technical prerequisites met on time? Did vendor support meet expectations? For **process changes**, assess whether the process itself was well-designed or whether adoption difficulties were actually design problems. For **restructuring**, the retrospective should address the human cost and whether it was managed with sufficient care and dignity — this shapes the organisation's future change culture.

todos.text: Identify the executive sponsor and any co-sponsors for this initiative
Schedule a 60–90 minute structured interview with each sponsor
Prepare the interview question set (see Questions below)
Send pre-read materials to the sponsor 48 hours before the interview (project brief, scope summary, expected role of sponsor)

Conduct the interview and document findings in a sponsor readiness matrix (using Prosci's CLARC model: Coalition building, Leadership, Active & visible participation, Resistance management, Communication)
Score sponsor readiness across each CLARC dimension and identify gaps
Develop a sponsor coaching plan to address identified gaps
[Project-specific: Add any additional sponsor-related tasks based on organisational structure, e.g., if there are multiple business unit sponsors who need aligning]
Obtain the organisational chart for all affected business units
Conduct a preliminary stakeholder brainstorm with the sponsor and project manager to identify all individuals and groups who are affected by, or can influence, the change
Categorise stakeholders by level: senior leaders, middle managers, frontline staff, support functions (IT, HR, L&D), external parties
Schedule structured interviews with senior leaders and their direct reports (30–45 minutes each)
Prepare the interview question set (see Questions below)
Conduct interviews and document each stakeholder's influence level and disposition (supportive, neutral, resistant)
Create a stakeholder map with axes of influence (high/low) and disposition (supportive/neutral/resistant)
Identify priority stakeholders: high-influence resistors (need converting) and high-influence supporters (need equipping)
[Project-specific: Add stakeholders unique to this project, e.g., union representatives, regulatory bodies, vendor partners]
Identify all distinct roles affected by the change (not just job titles — functional roles)
For each role, document the current-state workflow (how they do their work today)
For each role, document the future-state workflow (how they will do their work after the change)
Map the gap between current and future state for each role: what disappears, what stays, what is new, what changes
Create a structured impact analysis matrix: Role × Impact dimension (tools, processes, skills, reporting lines, performance metrics, workload)
Rate the severity of impact per role (high/medium/low) to prioritise intervention effort
Validate the impact analysis with middle managers and subject matter experts
[Project-specific: Add role-specific workflows and impact dimensions unique to the change being introduced]
Define what capabilities (skills, knowledge, behaviours) are relevant to assess for this change
Design or select an assessment instrument (survey, skills test, self-assessment, observation)
Pilot the assessment with a small group to validate clarity and relevance
Deploy the assessment across all affected populations
Analyse results and segment by role, department, location, and experience level
Identify capability clusters: those already near-ready, those needing moderate upskilling, those needing significant support
Document the baseline as a quantified reference point for measuring progress post-deployment
Share findings with the training architecture design (→ feeds into Activity 10)
[Project-specific: Define the specific skills and tools to assess based on what is being introduced]
Identify the major changes the organisation has undergone in the last 2–3 years
Select interviewees: long-tenured staff, middle managers, HR, and anyone who was involved in previous change efforts
Schedule 30–45 minute interviews (individual or small group)
Prepare the interview question set (see Questions below)
Conduct interviews and document themes, patterns, and emotional residue
Create a change history timeline noting: what was promised, what was delivered, what was the experience
Identify "scar tissue" — areas where previous failed or painful changes have created cynicism, distrust, or resistance patterns
Document implications for the current initiative: what to avoid, what to address explicitly, what narratives to counter
[Project-specific: Research specific past changes that are relevant to the current initiative, e.g., previous technology rollouts if this is a tech change]
Identify all middle managers whose teams are affected by the change
Schedule small group conversations (3–5 managers per group, 60 minutes) or individual interviews for sensitive contexts
Prepare the conversation guide (see Questions below)
Conduct conversations and document each manager's readiness (ability to support the change) and willingness (desire to support the change)
Plot managers on a readiness × willingness matrix
For managers who are willing but not ready: plan capability building (training, coaching, resources)
For managers who are ready but not willing: plan engagement and motivation interventions (address concerns, connect to their interests)
For managers who are neither ready nor willing: escalate to sponsor for direct intervention
Develop targeted support plans for each manager segment
[Project-specific: Add questions specific to the managerial capabilities this change requires, e.g., coaching digital skills if it's a tech rollout]
Compile findings from Activities 1–6 into a single working document
Identify all distinct affected populations (by role, department, location, or other meaningful segmentation)
For each population, analyse what they stand to lose: status, competence, autonomy, workload predictability
For each population, analyse what they might gain and whether they perceive it as a gain
Assess structural factors for each population: quality of manager support, sponsor visibility, communication trust, prior change experience
Rate resistance risk for each population (high/medium/low) with supporting evidence
Document the resistance risk profile as a formal artefact
Review and validate with the sponsor and programme manager
[Project-specific: Identify population-specific resistance factors unique to this change, e.g., for AI tools — fear of job replacement]
Draft the change strategy document structure: organisation context, change difficulty analysis, intervention plan, success criteria
Write the organisational context section: who is this organisation, what are its characteristics, how does it handle change?
Write the change difficulty analysis: what specifically makes this change hard for this organisation?
Define named populations with specific intervention plans for each (drawing from the resistance risk profile)
Define success criteria: what does successful adoption look like? At what levels? By when?
Define the measurement approach (high level — detailed measurement in Activity 13)
Include governance and escalation approach
Review with sponsor and obtain sign-off
[Project-specific: Tailor the strategy to the specific change type and organisational context]
Review the stakeholder map (from Activity 2) and resistance risk profile (from Activity 7)
Prioritise stakeholders by influence and disposition: identify the "must-engage" list
For each priority stakeholder, define the engagement approach: one-on-one, small group, or inclusion in a working group
Build a coalition of supportive stakeholders: equip them with talking points, early access, and information advantage
Plan early engagement with high-influence resistors — engage before the change becomes visible
Create an engagement cadence: who to meet, when, what's needed at each stage, what they need from you
Prepare talking points and briefing materials for supportive leaders to use with their teams
Schedule all engagements and set up calendar invitations
[Project-specific: Identify stakeholder-specific engagement needs, e.g., a union engagement process, regulatory briefings, vendor coordination meetings]
Map the gap between current capability (Activity 4) and required future-state capability (Activity 3) per role
Determine the training modality for each population: instructor-led, e-learning, peer coaching, self-paced, blended
Decide training timing: before deployment, at deployment, or phased after deployment
Define prerequisites: what must people know or have access to before training begins?
Map training touchpoints in the deployment timeline with dependencies
Identify who will deliver training: external trainers, internal L&D, champions, managers
Estimate training duration and scheduling constraints per population
Confirm training environment requirements (sandbox, test tenant, demo data)
Document the training architecture (structure and approach — not content yet)
[Project-specific: Design role-specific training pathways based on the specific tool, process, or change being introduced]
Define the communication audiences (may differ from stakeholder groups — broader reach)
Design the message sequence aligned to the audience's psychological journey:
Define the communication channels for each audience (email, Teams, town hall, intranet, manager cascade, video)
Ensure managers hear before their teams — build in a management pre-brief at each stage
Create a communication timeline mapped to the deployment schedule
Draft key messages and have them reviewed by the sponsor and programme manager
Plan for two-way communication: how will questions and feedback be captured and responded to?
[Project-specific: Adapt messaging to address the specific "What's in it for me?" for each population and the specific change being introduced]
Obtain the full technical deployment timeline from the IT/technical project manager
Map every technical milestone and identify its downstream ACM implications (e.g., "tenant configured" → "training environments available")
Confirm pilot environment availability dates and scope
Confirm go-live date and any hard dependencies (e.g., licence activation, security approvals)
Identify the rollback plan: what happens if go-live fails?
Build the ACM plan against the technical timeline — align all communication, training, and support activities to technical milestones
Identify risks: what technical delays would cascade into ACM plan disruption?
Establish a regular sync cadence with the technical team
[Project-specific: Map technology-specific deployment steps and their ACM implications]
Define metrics at three levels:
Identify data sources for each metric: system telemetry, surveys, assessments, business data
Establish baseline measurements (drawing from Activity 4)
Define measurement frequency: daily/weekly during deployment, monthly post-deployment
Partner with business stakeholders to agree on business impact metrics and data access
Build or configure dashboards/reports for ongoing monitoring
Integrate the measurement framework into the change strategy document
[Project-specific: Define specific metrics based on the tool, process, or change and available data sources]
Review the resistance risk profile (from Activity 7)
For each high-risk resistance cluster, design a specific intervention:
Distinguish between resistance that is an adoption problem (to be managed) and resistance that signals a genuine design/structural problem (to be escalated)
Prepare intervention materials and resources
Brief the sponsor, middle managers, and champions on resistance patterns to expect and how to respond
Define escalation criteria: what level of resistance triggers escalation to governance?
[Project-specific: Design interventions tailored to the specific resistance patterns expected for this change]
Define the champion role: expectations, time commitment, benefits, and duration
Define champion selection criteria: peer credibility, enthusiasm for the change, respected by colleagues (not just high performers or volunteers)
Identify potential champions — solicit nominations from middle managers and the sponsor
Conduct a formal recruitment process: approach candidates individually, explain the role, gauge willingness
Secure manager approval for each champion's time commitment
Equip champions: provide early access to the tool/process, deeper context on the change, and a direct communication line back to the ACM team
Conduct initial champion training: what they need to know, how to support peers, how to report back
Establish a regular check-in rhythm (weekly during deployment, fortnightly post-deployment)
Create a champion communication channel (e.g., Teams channel)
Prepare updated talking points and troubleshooting guidance for champions to use
[Project-specific: Tailor champion role and training to the specific change, e.g., tool-specific tips for tech rollouts]
Identify the existing programme governance structure (steering committee, project board, working groups)
Propose a formal seat for ACM in programme governance (standing agenda item, reporting cadence)
Define which decisions the ACM lead can make autonomously vs. what requires governance approval
Define what constitutes an ACM risk requiring escalation (e.g., adoption dropping below threshold, widespread resistance, sponsor disengagement)
Agree on ACM reporting format and frequency
Negotiate ACM inclusion in the programme risk register
Document governance agreements and circulate to all governance members
[Project-specific: Adapt governance integration to the specific programme structure and political dynamics]
Create or request creation of the following Teams channels:
Define the purpose and posting guidelines for each channel
Populate channels with relevant members
Post a welcome message in each channel explaining its purpose
Pin key resources (project brief, timeline, FAQ) in each channel
Assign channel moderation responsibilities
[Project-specific: Create additional channels if needed, e.g., regional channels for multi-location deployments, or topic-specific channels for complex changes]
Confirm the deployment date is firm with the technical team
Send pre-deployment communication 48–72 hours before go-live
Ensure managers have been briefed before any communication reaches their teams
Ensure champions know what benefits they're receiving and what's expected of them
Activate two-way communication channels (Q&A channels, feedback forms)
Monitor initial reactions and address misinformation or confusion rapidly
Send day-one communication: practical "what to do now" guidance
[Project-specific: Customise messaging with specific tool/process instructions and "What's in it for me" for each audience]
Confirm training schedule with all participants and their managers
Ensure training environments and materials are ready
Deliver training in the designed sequence: awareness → skill building → applied practice
Collect participant feedback after each training session
Track attendance and follow up with non-attendees
Provide post-training reference materials and quick-start guides
Identify participants who are struggling and arrange additional support
Feed training observations into the resistance management approach
[Project-specific: Execute role-specific training based on the training architecture designed in Activity 10]
Brief champions on their active support role during deployment
Position champions for peer-level help within their teams (less stigma than formal IT/training support)
Establish champion intelligence-gathering protocol: what to observe, how to report back
Champions model productive use of the new tool/process visibly within their teams
Maintain daily or every-other-day champion check-ins during the first two weeks
Collect and synthesise champion intelligence: what's working, what's confusing, what's failing
Ensure intelligence reaches the ACM team within hours, not days
Act on champion intelligence — adjust support, communication, or training as needed
[Project-specific: Define specific support activities and intelligence-gathering focus based on the change type]
Confirm dedicated help desk queue is operational and staffed
Set up drop-in clinic schedule (physical or virtual) — floor-walking support if applicable
Ensure Teams support channels are monitored and responsive
Brief support staff on common issues and resolution paths
Ensure all support is visibly operational from the moment access goes live
Over-resource support in the first week; scale back based on demand data
Track support ticket volume, categories, and resolution times
Feed support data into the rapid check-act loops (Activity 24)
[Project-specific: Configure support infrastructure for the specific tool/process and known technical issues]
Activate monitoring dashboards and reports as designed in Activity 13
Begin tracking adoption metrics from day one of deployment
Compare daily/weekly metrics against the baseline established in Activity 4
Track utilisation metrics: who is using it, how often, what features
Track proficiency indicators: are people using it correctly?
Identify cohorts that are adopting well vs. cohorts that are lagging
Report findings to the programme governance at agreed frequency
Feed data into rapid check-act loops (Activity 24)
[Project-specific: Configure specific telemetry and dashboards for the tool or process being deployed]
Activate the resistance management interventions designed in Activity 14
Monitor for pre-identified resistance patterns manifesting as actual behaviour
Distinguish between productive resistance (identifying genuine problems → escalate to technical team) and defensive resistance (protecting established habits or status → manage through ACM)
Deploy specific interventions for each resistance cluster as designed
Ensure the sponsor is actively visible during the first two weeks — attending floor walks, sending messages, checking in with teams
Coach the sponsor on resistance interactions: what to say, how to respond, when to listen
Track resistance patterns: is resistance decreasing, stable, or escalating?
Adjust interventions if initial approach is not working
[Project-specific: Deploy interventions specific to the resistance patterns identified for this change]
Synthesise findings from all sensing channels: champion reports, support tickets, telemetry data, resistance observations, manager feedback
Identify the most significant gaps between expected and actual adoption within the first week
Diagnose probable causes for each gap
Design adjustments: modified training, additional communication, targeted support, process change, escalation
Implement adjustments within the same week
Document all changes and rationale in a change log
Report adjustments and outcomes to governance
Repeat the cycle: synthesise → diagnose → design → implement → document
[Project-specific: Define the specific data sources and synthesis cadence for this project's check-act loops]
Conduct a structured review of the champion network: who is still active? Who has disengaged?
Assess each champion's own adoption level — are they practising what they preach?
Gather feedback from champions: what worked, what was difficult, what support did they lack?
Identify burnout risk — champions often carry extra load during deployment
Recognise and thank active champions (publicly and privately)
Decide whether the champion network should continue, be refreshed, or be wound down
If continuing: recruit replacement champions for those who have disengaged
[Project-specific: Assess champion effectiveness against the specific support outcomes expected for this change]
Synthesise data from all sources: telemetry, interview findings, training effectiveness, champion reports, support ticket trends, resistance pattern evolution, sponsor engagement quality
Assess progress against the success criteria defined in the change strategy (Activity 8)
Identify what is working well and should be continued or amplified
Identify what is not working and needs adjustment
Create a coherent written assessment as a formal deliverable (not a verbal update)
Present to the steering committee
Obtain governance decisions on any recommended adjustments
[Project-specific: Include project-specific data sources and success criteria in the review]
Segment the user population by adoption maturity: advanced users, competent users, struggling users, non-users
For advanced users: design deepening activities (advanced training, power-user communities, use-case showcases)
For competent users: design reinforcement (refresher sessions, tips-and-tricks, continued champion support)
For struggling users: design remedial support (one-on-one coaching, simplified guides, manager intervention)
For non-users: diagnose root cause and decide whether to apply pressure, provide support, or accept non-adoption
Schedule reinforcement activities and assign ownership
Set a review date to assess reinforcement effectiveness
[Project-specific: Tailor reinforcement content to specific tool features, process steps, or skills relevant to this change]
Review all accumulated data for issues that are design, policy, or infrastructure problems — not adoption problems
Categorise barriers: technical configuration, security/compliance, process design, policy, organisational structure
For each barrier: document the evidence, the impact on adoption, and a proposed resolution
Escalate to the appropriate owner (IT, HR, business leadership, vendor) with specific evidence
Track escalated barriers to resolution — do not let them disappear into a queue
Report barrier status to governance
[Project-specific: Identify the specific systemic barriers likely to emerge for this type of change]
Identify internal owners for ongoing adoption management:
Create a knowledge transfer plan: what to transfer, to whom, by when
Transfer the measurement framework and dashboards with instructions
Transfer the champion network management approach: how to recruit, brief, and support champions
Transfer stakeholder relationships: introduce internal owners to key stakeholders
Hand over all documentation: change strategy, resistance profile, communication materials, training content
Conduct structured handover sessions — not just document drops
Shadow period: internal owners operate with consultant available for questions
[Project-specific: Identify specific knowledge assets and relationship transfers needed for this project]
Revisit the original resistance risk profile (Activity 7) and compare predictions to actual outcomes
Document which resistance patterns resolved, which transformed, and which persisted
Identify new resistance patterns that emerged and were not predicted
Distinguish between resistance that was validly managed as an adoption issue and resistance that proved to be a genuine design/structural problem
Update the resistance profile to reflect current state
Design ongoing management interventions for any persistent resistance
Document lessons for future initiatives: what resistance predictions were accurate and which were wrong?
[Project-specific: Assess resistance patterns specific to this change type and their current status]
Identify successful patterns and behaviours that need to be structurally embedded (not dependent on project energy or champion effort)
Update standard operating procedures (SOPs) to reflect new ways of working
Update job descriptions and competency frameworks to include new skills and expectations
Update onboarding programmes so new joiners learn the new way from day one
Transfer training materials to the learning management system (LMS) with an identified owner and a content refresh cycle
Embed adoption metrics into regular business reporting (not just project reporting)
Apply the "bus test": if everyone involved in this project left tomorrow, would the successful patterns persist?
Close gaps identified by the bus test
[Project-specific: Identify the specific processes, documents, and systems that need updating for this change]
Schedule a retrospective session with the core ACM team, programme manager, and key stakeholders
Prepare the retrospective agenda: what went well, what didn't, what we'd do differently, lessons for the organisation
Assess each major ACM intervention: was it the right intervention? Was it effective?
Document time and effort spent on each phase and activity (to inform scoping of future projects)
Identify lessons that should inform the organisation's approach to future change initiatives
Create a formal retrospective document and share with the sponsor and governance
Archive all project materials (change strategy, resistance profiles, measurement data, communication materials) for future reference
[Project-specific: Include project-specific reflections on what was unique about this change and what was transferable]
Set up shared document repository with appropriate access permissions
Agree governance rhythm with sponsor (status updates, steering committee cadence)
Create project tracker and RAID log
Set up recurring meeting cadence with key stakeholders
Establish reporting template and cadence
Define escalation paths and decision-making authority
Manage consultant access to client systems and tools
Get organisational chart for all affected business units
Get list of affected populations and user groups
Get contact list with name, function, department, and email
Get access to relevant internal systems and platforms
Get prior change documentation or lessons learned
Get project charter, scope document, or business case
Get list of existing communication channels and distribution lists

questions.question_text: What does success look like to you personally for this initiative?
Why is this change important now? What happens if we don't do it?
How does this initiative connect to the broader organisational strategy?
What are you personally willing to do visibly to support this change?
What resources (budget, people, time) are you prepared to allocate?
What will you defend when competing priorities emerge?
Where does this rank among your current priorities?
What do you see as the biggest risks to adoption?
Which groups do you expect to resist, and why?
How have you handled change resistance in previous initiatives?
Who else in the leadership team actively supports this?
Are there leaders who are sceptical or opposed? Who?
Who do people in the organisation actually listen to, regardless of title?
How do you currently communicate major changes to the organisation?
Are you willing to send communications, attend town halls, and record video messages?
What is your role, and how does your team interact with the area being changed?
How many people report to you directly and indirectly?
Who else should I be speaking to about this change?
What have you heard about this initiative so far?
What is your initial reaction — what excites you and what concerns you?
On a scale of 1–10, how supportive are you of this change? What would move you higher?
When you express a view about a change, who listens?
Who do your people go to for advice when they're uncertain about something new?
questions.ask_whom: Each identified stakeholder / Ask: Their direct reports

questions.question_text: Are there informal leaders in your area whose opinion carries weight? Who?
What would you need from us to feel confident supporting this change?
How do you prefer to be kept informed — and how often?
Walk me through a typical day/week in your role — what tools do you use and what tasks do you perform?
questions.ask_whom: End Users / Frontline Staff (sample per role)

questions.question_text: Which parts of your current workflow are most time-consuming or frustrating?
What workarounds have you developed to get things done?
What have you been told about how your work will change?
What aspects of the change are unclear to you?
Which of your team's workflows will be most affected by this change?
Will any tasks or responsibilities disappear entirely? Which ones?
What new tasks or skills will your team need to acquire?
Will performance metrics or KPIs change as a result?
Will reporting lines or team structures change?
Does your team have capacity to learn new ways of working alongside their current workload?
What time of year or business cycle would make this change hardest to absorb?
How would you rate your proficiency with the current tools/processes being replaced or augmented?
What formal training have you received on your current tools in the last 12 months?
What do you find most difficult about your current tools or processes?
questions.ask_whom: End Users (via survey or focus group)

questions.question_text: How do you prefer to learn new skills — classroom, online, peer coaching, self-directed?
How confident are you in your ability to learn new technology or processes?
What has your experience been with previous training programmes — were they helpful?
Does the organisation have an existing learning management system (LMS) or training infrastructure?
What training resources (trainers, rooms, e-learning platforms) are available?
Are there existing digital champions or super-users who could be leveraged?
What significant changes has this organisation gone through in the last 2–3 years?
Which of those changes went well? What made them successful?
Which changes were painful or failed? What went wrong?
When leadership announces a change, what is the typical reaction on the ground?
Has there been a change where promises were made but not kept? What happened?
Do people generally trust that changes are in their interest, or is there scepticism?
How are changes typically communicated here — and is it effective?
Is there a pattern of "change fatigue" — too many changes at once?
Have previous changes been properly completed, or do people feel like changes were abandoned halfway?
Has the organisation attempted anything similar to this change before? What happened?
questions.ask_whom: Middle Managers / IT Lead / Programme Manager

questions.question_text: Are there specific groups or departments where change is particularly difficult? Why?
What do you understand about this change and why it's happening?
What questions do you have that haven't been answered?
Do you feel you have enough information to explain this change to your team?
How do you feel about this change personally — is it something you believe in?
What concerns do you have about the impact on your team?
Are you willing to actively advocate for this change with your team, even when it's difficult?
What would make you more confident or enthusiastic about supporting this?
Have you led your team through a significant change before? How did it go?
Do you feel equipped to support your team through this transition — training, coaching, handling resistance?
What support or resources would you need from us to lead this effectively?
Do you and your team have capacity to take on this change alongside current workload?
What competing priorities might get in the way?
How will you know if your team is struggling with the change?
What status or recognition might this group lose? (e.g., being the expert, having a unique skill)
What competence will they lose — will they go from expert to novice?
What autonomy or control over their work might they lose?
Will their workload increase during or after the transition?
Will the predictability of their work decrease?
What tangible benefits will this group experience?
Do they believe these benefits are real? Have they been communicated?
When will they start experiencing the benefits — immediately or after a difficult transition period?
How supportive is this group's direct manager of the change?
Has this group experienced broken promises from previous changes?
Does this group trust organisational communications?
What is the compelling case for this change? Why now?
What are the top three risks to successful adoption?
What is the single biggest obstacle we need to overcome?
How will we know this change has been successfully adopted?
What adoption metrics matter most — utilisation, proficiency, or business impact?
What is the timeline for achieving full adoption?
What level of adoption is the minimum acceptable?
For each population at high resistance risk: what specific intervention will address their concerns?
What resources are available for interventions?
Are there organisational constraints on the types of interventions we can use? (e.g., no mandatory training, no town halls, unionised environments)
For each key stakeholder: what do they need to know, feel, and do at each project stage?
What is the right frequency of engagement for each stakeholder?
Are there stakeholders who need to be engaged together (because they influence each other) or separately (because of political dynamics)?
Which supportive stakeholders have the most credibility with resistant groups?
What do supportive stakeholders need from us to advocate effectively?
Are supportive stakeholders willing to speak publicly at town halls or in communications?
For each high-influence resistor: what is the root cause of their resistance?
Is their resistance based on legitimate concerns that need addressing, or on personal interests?
Who is the best person to engage each resistor — the sponsor, a peer, or the ACM consultant?
What training modalities does the organisation already use and trust?
Are there scheduling constraints — can people be taken off their work for training? For how long?
Is there a training environment or sandbox available for hands-on practice?
Can training be recorded for later on-demand access?
For each affected role: what are the critical skills they must have from day one vs. what can be learned over time?
Are there accessibility requirements (language, disability, remote workers)?
Are there populations who will need significantly more support than others?
How many people need to be trained, and over what timeframe?
Can training be delivered in the existing meeting/training infrastructure, or do we need additional facilities?
What is the budget for training materials and delivery?
What communication channels are most effective in this organisation?
Are there populations who don't regularly check email or intranet? How do we reach them?
Is there an internal communications team we should coordinate with?
Does the organisation have existing communication templates or brand guidelines we must follow?
What is the core "Why?" — the compelling reason for this change that will resonate with staff?
What are the top three concerns or questions staff are likely to have?
What is the "What's in it for me?" for each audience?
questions.ask_whom: Synthesise from Activity 3 / Ask: Programme Manager

questions.question_text: Are there messages from previous changes that need to be explicitly countered or addressed?
How far in advance of deployment should the first communication go out?
Is there a management cascade process we can use — or do we need to create one?
How will we handle rumours or misinformation?
What is the confirmed deployment timeline with key milestones?
Which milestones are firm vs. tentative?
What are the dependencies between technical milestones?
When will the pilot environment be ready for training and testing?
Which groups get access first, and in what sequence?
What features or capabilities will be available at pilot vs. full deployment?
Are there any technical limitations that affect the user experience at launch?
What is the rollback plan if deployment fails?
What are the most likely causes of delay?
How will we be notified of technical issues during deployment?
Is there a deployment freeze window (e.g., financial year-end) we need to avoid?
What system telemetry or usage data is available?
What business metrics should improve as a result of this change?
How will we measure whether people are using the new tool/process correctly, not just at all?
Are there existing reports or dashboards we can leverage?
Who owns the data we need, and will they share it?
Are there data privacy considerations (GDPR, etc.) we need to address?
What is the reporting cadence expected by the steering committee?
What are the current values of the metrics we plan to track?
questions.ask_whom: Synthesise from Activity 4 / Ask: IT Lead / Business Intelligence

questions.question_text: Are there seasonal or cyclical variations we need to account for?
For each resistance cluster: is this resistance about fear, loss, lack of understanding, or a genuine problem?
What safe practice environments can we create for people anxious about competence?
Who are the best people to have one-on-one conversations with resistors — managers, sponsors, peers, or the consultant?
Are there structural barriers (policy, technology configuration, process design) creating legitimate resistance that should be escalated rather than managed?
questions.ask_whom: Consultant assessment / Ask: Middle Managers / IT Lead

questions.question_text: How should managers respond when a team member openly resists?
questions.ask_whom: Consultant design / Review with: HR / Middle Managers

questions.question_text: At what point does individual resistance become a performance issue? Who decides?
How will we track resistance patterns during deployment to know if our interventions are working?
Who in each team is respected by their peers and has informal influence?
Who has shown enthusiasm or early interest in this change?
Are there people who were champions in previous change initiatives? Were they effective?
How many champions do we need per team/department/location?
questions.ask_whom: Consultant judgement based on population size

questions.question_text: How much time per week can champions realistically dedicate?
What recognition or incentive can we offer champions?
What early access or special resources can we provide to champions?
What training do champions need to fulfil their role effectively?
How will champions report back what they're hearing on the ground?
What happens if a champion disengages or leaves?
What is the existing governance structure for this programme?
Who sits on the steering committee, and how often does it meet?
Is there currently an ACM voice in governance, or will this be new?
What decisions can the ACM lead make without governance approval? (e.g., adjusting communication timing, modifying training approach)
What requires escalation? (e.g., budget reallocation, scope changes, sponsor intervention requests)
How quickly can governance respond to escalated ACM risks?
What reporting format does governance expect?
How frequently should ACM report — same cadence as technical, or different?
Does governance want leading indicators (risk signals) or lagging indicators (adoption data)?
Does the organisation use Microsoft Teams as its collaboration platform? If not, what do they use?
Are there any restrictions on creating Teams channels (e.g., IT approval required)?
Are there existing channels or communication spaces we should use rather than creating new ones?
Who should have access to each channel?
questions.ask_whom: Consultant design / Confirm with: Programme Manager

questions.question_text: Who will moderate each channel and respond to questions?
questions.ask_whom: Consultant design / Negotiate with: Programme Manager

questions.question_text: Should channels be private or discoverable?
Is the deployment date confirmed? Are there any remaining blockers?
Have all managers been briefed?
Are champions equipped and ready?
Are support channels operational?
questions.ask_whom: Check with: Help Desk / IT Lead

questions.question_text: What is the first thing users will see or experience when the change goes live?
What is the single most important thing users need to know on day one?
What common questions or issues should we pre-empt in the day-one communication?
Are all training sessions scheduled and confirmed?
questions.ask_whom: Check with: L&D / Programme Manager / Middle Managers

questions.question_text: Are trainers prepared and briefed on the training content and approach?
Is the training environment functional and loaded with realistic data?
After each session: what was clear? What was confusing? What do you need more practice on?
Do participants feel ready to use the new tool/process in their real work?
What questions are coming up repeatedly that suggest a gap in training content?
Are all champions active and engaged?
Are champions themselves confident and proficient with the change?
Are managers supporting champion time commitment as agreed?
What are the most common questions or issues people are raising?
Are there specific teams, roles, or locations struggling more than others?
Are people reverting to old tools or processes? Where and why?
Is there any resistance that is escalating or spreading?
Is the help desk queue configured and staffed?
Do support staff know about the change and the common issues users will face?
Are drop-in clinics or floor-walking sessions scheduled for the first two weeks?
How many support requests do we anticipate in the first week?
Do we have enough support capacity, or do we need additional resources?
What is the escalation path for issues that support cannot resolve?
Is the telemetry data flowing correctly?
Are the metrics we're seeing reliable, or are there data quality issues?
Can we segment the data by team, department, role, and location?
What does the adoption data tell us — who is adopting and who isn't?
Are the patterns aligned with our resistance risk profile predictions?
Are there surprises — populations doing better or worse than expected?
What resistance behaviours are we actually seeing? Do they match predictions?
Is the resistance concentrated in specific groups, or distributed?
Are there new resistance patterns we didn't predict?
Are the planned interventions working? What's shifting and what isn't?
Does the sponsor need to intervene directly with any specific groups or individuals?
Are there structural barriers causing legitimate resistance that need escalation?
What are the top three issues emerging across all sensing channels?
Are these issues localised or systemic?
Do they require ACM adjustment, technical escalation, or both?
What is the fastest intervention we can deploy for each issue?
Who needs to approve or execute the adjustment?
questions.ask_whom: Depends on issue: Programme Manager / IT Lead / Sponsor

questions.question_text: How will we know if the adjustment worked?
How many of our original champions are still actively participating?
How are champions feeling — energised, neutral, or burnt out?
Are champions still getting time from their managers to fulfil the role?
Which champions were most effective, and what made them so?
Were there teams where having a champion made a visible difference to adoption?
questions.ask_whom: Consultant analysis of adoption data by team

questions.question_text: Did champions provide useful intelligence that led to actionable adjustments?
Is there ongoing need for a champion network, or can support now shift to BAU?
Would champions be willing to continue in a reduced capacity?
Are we on track against the success criteria we set?
What is the overall adoption health: green, amber, or red?
Are there populations where adoption has stalled or failed? What do we do about it?
Do we need to adjust the change strategy based on what we've learned?
Are there resources or interventions we need that weren't in the original plan?
Is the deployment timeline still realistic, or do we need to adjust?
questions.ask_whom: Discuss with: Programme Manager / IT Lead

questions.question_text: What does the adoption data tell us about who needs what?
Are there patterns in who is struggling — role, location, manager, tenure?
Are the struggling users struggling with motivation, capability, or structural barriers?
What training modalities worked best during initial training?
Are advanced users willing to become mentors or showcase their use cases?
What additional content or resources would help struggling users?
Are there issues people are experiencing that no amount of training or communication will fix?
Are there security or configuration restrictions preventing people from using the tool/process as intended?
Are there policy or process constraints that conflict with the new way of working?
Who is the right owner for each barrier?
What evidence is needed to make a compelling case for resolution?
What is the expected timeline for resolution?
Who will own ongoing adoption management after the consultant exits? Is this person identified and engaged?
Does the identified owner have capacity and authority for this role?
Is ongoing adoption management resourced (budget, time), or is it expected to be absorbed into existing roles?
Does the internal owner understand the measurement framework and how to use it?
Does the internal owner have relationships with key stakeholders, or do introductions need to be made?
Are there any ACM activities that still require external expertise and cannot be fully transferred?
Which resistance patterns have resolved? What resolved them?
questions.ask_whom: Consultant analysis / Ask: Champions / Middle Managers

questions.question_text: Which resistance patterns persist? Why?
questions.ask_whom: Consultant analysis / Ask: Champions / Middle Managers

questions.question_text: Has any resistance transformed — e.g., moved from active resistance to passive non-adoption?
Have new resistance patterns emerged that we didn't predict?
Looking back, which resistance was genuinely about adoption (and correctly managed by ACM) and which was signalling a real problem (that should have been escalated sooner)?
Are there persistent resistors whose concerns have been validated by outcomes?
What new ways of working need to be reflected in SOPs?
Do job descriptions need updating? Who has authority to change them?
Does the onboarding programme reflect the new way of working?
Are training materials housed in a permanent system with an owner and refresh schedule?
If we removed all project support tomorrow, would the new ways of working persist?
What would revert first — and how do we prevent that?
Are the right incentives and accountability structures in place to sustain the change?
Which ACM interventions made the biggest difference? How do we know?
questions.ask_whom: Core ACM team / Programme Manager / Champions

questions.question_text: Which interventions didn't work as expected? Why?
Were there interventions we should have done but didn't?
How long did each phase take? Was the original timeline realistic?
Where did we spend the most effort? Was it the right place?
What would we do differently if we ran the same project again?
What has this project taught us about how this organisation handles change?
What should the organisation invest in to make future changes easier? (e.g., internal change capability, manager development, communication infrastructure)
Are there transferable frameworks, templates, or approaches from this project that should be standardised?
